text: The image of the lone genius — Mozart composing at age four, Einstein intuiting relativity on a tram — has always been seductive and largely fictional. Michael Howe's study of biographical records found that even those classified as prodigies showed the same pattern Ericsson identified in ordinary experts: years of concentrated, guided practice before any notable achievement. Mozart's early compositions, his father Leopold's letters reveal, were heavily corrected and supervised. Einstein's special relativity paper, published at twenty-six, followed years of intense thought experiments he had conducted since adolescence. The genius myth flatters observers and discourages learners. The deliberate practice model is less romantic but far more useful: it offers a mechanism, not a mystery.
question: What does the passage cite as evidence that Mozart's early compositions do not support the lone-genius myth?

options.text: Mozart's early works were later found to have been composed collaboratively with other musicians
His father Leopold's letters reveal the compositions were heavily corrected and supervised
Musicologists have dated his first original works to age eight, not age four as traditionally claimed
His famous early pieces were arrangements of existing melodies rather than original compositions
